In Ibrahim’s final moments, the Prophet sat beside him, and while tears flowed from his eyes, he said, "Although parting with you is very painful and my heart is deeply grieved and my eyes are full of tears, I am content with the divine decree and say nothing but what pleases Allah."
When they were shrouding the small body of the Prophet’s son, the Prophet said, "Let me see him one last time." He then leaned over Ibrahim's body and wept so intensely that his shoulders and chin trembled with grief. One of the companions, seeing the Prophet in such a state, asked, "O Messenger of Allah! You are a prophet! Why are you so distressed?" The Prophet replied, "This is a mercy and compassion that Allah places in the hearts of whomever He wills. Allah has mercy on His servants who have mercy and compassion in their hearts."
Ali (AS) placed Ibrahim’s body in a coffin and carried it on a horse to the Baqi' cemetery in Medina. The Messenger of Allah (PBUH), along with the people, prayed over him and, with a strained voice, said, "In Paradise, there will be someone to nurse Ibrahim." During the burial, Ali (AS) entered the grave, leveled it, and buried Ibrahim. At this moment, the Prophet wept intensely. The Muslims, moved by his grief, wept so much that their cries were heard. The Prophet requested a water skin, and he sprinkled some water on the grave. Then he placed a stone marker on Ibrahim's grave.
Maria, unable to bear the separation from the Prophet and her son, passed away in the month of Muharram, five years after the Prophet's demise, in Mashrabat. She was also buried in Baqi' beside her son Ibrahim's grave. After her burial, the area around her grave became the burial site for other wives of the Prophet.
Up until the time of Imam Sadiq (AS), Mashrabat was preserved in its original state. However, after that, it was converted into a mosque and gradually became known among the people as the Mosque of Mashrabat. After the destruction of the tombs in the Baqi' cemetery, the Mosque of Mashrabat was also demolished and turned into a cemetery. Unfortunately, today there is no trace of Mashrabat, the Prophet's residence, or the mosque. Mashrabat Umm Ibrahim is located between two hospitals on Ali ibn Abi Talib Street in Medina, approximately 500 meters away from Al-Zahra Hospital.

Rukn al-Yamani or the Southern Corner is one of the corners of the Kaaba, located towards the south and in the direction of Yemen. It is considered the most virtuous corner of the Kaaba after the Rukn al-Hajar al-Aswad (the Black Stone corner), and in narrations, it is described as one of the gates of Paradise.
Recommended acts regarding Rukn al-Yamani include touching it, kissing it, embracing it, and reciting salawat (blessings on the Prophet), supplication, and repentance near it.
According to some Shi'a scholars, the spot where the wall of the Kaaba split open to allow Fatima bint Asad (the mother of Imam Ali (a)) to enter, was beside the Rukn al-Yamani.

The Shami Corner or Western Corner is one of the corners of the Kaaba, located towards the west. A person performing Tawaf reaches this corner after passing the Hijr Ismail area. It is also called the Shami Corner, though some researchers state that this naming lacks historical precedent.
In hadith and jurisprudential sources, it is recommended to touch (Istilam), kiss, and supplicate at all the corners of the Kaaba, including the Western Corner.
The term Rukn means pillar or foundational corner in architecture. Since there are four pillars inside the Kaaba holding up its ceiling, each of the Kaaba’s four corners is called a Rukn.
The Western Corner is located on the west side of the Kaaba, and the person performing Tawaf reaches it after passing Hijr Ismail.
The distance between this corner and the Yemeni Corner (Rukn Yamani) is about 12 meters.

The Northern Corner, or Iraqi Corner, is one of the four corners of the Kaaba, located approximately towards the north of Arabia, in the direction of Iraq. A person performing Tawaf (circumambulation) reaches the Hijr Ismail area after passing this corner.
The door of the Kaaba is situated between this corner and the Black Stone (Hajar al-Aswad) Corner.
In hadith and Islamic jurisprudence sources, it is recommended to touch (Istilam), kiss, and supplicate near all the corners of the Kaaba, including the Northern Corner.
The word Rukn in architecture means a pillar or foundational corner. Since there are four pillars inside the Kaaba holding up the ceiling at each of its four corners, each of these corners is referred to as a Rukn.

The lineage of Muhammad (PBUH), the son of Abdullah bin Abdul-Muttalib, traces back to Ishmael (AS), the son of Abraham (AS), and ultimately to Adam (AS). His mother, Amina bint Wahb bin Abd Manaf, belonged to the Banu Zuhra clan of the Quraysh tribe, and the paternal and maternal lines of his parents converge at Abd Manaf. Amina was renowned for her nobility and chastity, being considered the most esteemed woman among the Banu Zuhra, to the extent that she was regarded as the foremost noblewoman of the Quraysh. At a time when having a daughter was seen as a source of shame among the Arabs, she held a special place of respect within her family. While Amina was pregnant, Abdullah, her husband, joined other Qurayshi merchants on a trade journey to Syria and fell ill upon his return. He passed away in Yathrib at the age of twenty-five, never having the opportunity to see his newborn son.

The father, mother, and ancestors of the Prophet (PBUH) were all believers in the one true God. The Prophet (PBUH) is reported to have said, "God always transferred me from the loins of pure fathers to the wombs of pure mothers and never allowed me to be sullied by the filth of ignorance." Imam Ali (AS) also said about the ancestors of the Prophet (PBUH), "God chose Muhammad (PBUH) from the lineage of the prophets, from the family of the best, from the land of Batha, and from among the glowing lamps in the darkness and the fountains of wisdom." Imam Sadiq (AS) narrated that the Prophet (PBUH) said, "Abdul-Muttalib never worshipped idols and followed the religion of his father Abraham."

When the Prophet (PBUH) was born, Amina (SA) heard a voice saying, "Show Muhammad to the East and West and present him to the jinn and humans, birds, and beasts. Grant him the purity of Adam (AS), the tenderness of Noah (AS), the friendship of Abraham (AS), the language of Ishmael (AS), the perfection of Joseph (AS), the joy of Jacob (AS), the voice of David (AS), the asceticism of John (AS), and the generosity of Jesus (AS)." Imam Sadiq (AS) also narrated, "When the Prophet (PBUH) was born, the palaces of Persia and the castles of Syria were illuminated for Amina by a light that shone."

According to the majority of Shia scholars, Muhammad (PBUH) was born on Friday, the 17th of Rabi' al-Awwal in the Year of the Elephant (the year when the army of Abraha, accompanied by elephants, came to destroy the Kaaba), which corresponds to 570 AD in Mecca. The Prophet (PBUH) is reported to have said, "I am the result of my father Abraham's (AS) prayer and the one whose coming Jesus (AS) gave glad tidings of."

The birth of the Prophet (PBUH) brought about remarkable changes across the world, unprecedented before. Imam Sadiq (AS) narrated, "At the time of the Prophet's birth, the arches of the palace of Khosrow in Ctesiphon trembled and several of its spires fell. The fire temple of Persia, which had been burning for a thousand years, was extinguished. The lake of Sawa dried up. Idols fell on their faces. No king remained whose crown did not topple, and on that day, their tongues were silenced. The magic of magicians was nullified and soothsayers lost their knowledge. With the birth of Jesus (AS), demons were barred from three heavens, and with the birth of the Prophet (PBUH), they were banned from all seven heavens. On that night, a light emerged from the land of Hijaz that spread to the East."

According to popular reports, the Prophet (PBUH) was born in a house in the Sha'b of Abi Talib, which became known as "Mawlid al-Nabi," the birthplace of the Prophet. When the Prophet (PBUH) migrated to Medina, this house was in the possession of Aqil, the son of Abu Talib, and later belonged to his descendants. Eventually, Khayzuran, the wife of Harun al-Rashid, purchased it. Muslims revered this place, especially holding ceremonies there on the night of the Prophet's birth. Despite historical challenges and changes, the birthplace of the Prophet continued to function as a small mosque for centuries and was maintained and renovated over the years. Reports from the sixth century onwards indicate that a marble monument adorned the site where the Prophet (PBUH) was born, and in the reconstruction in 1009 AH, a large dome and minaret were added to the mosque.

Many historians, in their travels to Mecca, have described this place in detail in their travelogues, portraying it as a magnificent mosque that "opens on Mondays in the month of Rabi' al-Awwal, the month of the Prophet's birth, and people flock there to gain blessings, creating a scene of resurrection in Mecca on that day." The mosque is described as a square structure with two arches and a high dome, with its interior decorated with a valuable carpet, a pulpit covered with silk fabrics, and a gilded door.

In 1343 AH (1924 AD), the birthplace of the Prophet was demolished, but due to the insistence of the then mayor of Mecca, it was decided to build a library there, now known as "Maktabat Makkah al-Mukarramah." This site is located near the Masjid al-Haram at the end of Bab al-Safa and is paved with stone leading to the Mas'a. The library houses a vast collection of manuscripts, rare local and Islamic newspapers, and publications. This building is the only structure remaining in the open space behind the Mas'a, facing Mount Abu Qubais. Many scholars and prominent figures of Mecca have donated their books to this library, which are kept in specific sections named after them. A book has also been published about this library and its history. This book contains a painted picture of the old building of Mawlid al-Nabi, which appears to be unique, and unfortunately, apart from that, no photographs of the ancient structure of the Prophet's birthplace are available.

Al-Mustajar (Arabic: المستجار) is a part of the western wall of the Ka'ba, approximately 2 meters in length, located between the al-Rukn al-Yamani and the second door of the Ka'ba, which was sealed during the time of al-Hajjaj b. Yusuf al-Thaqafi. This section is at the back of the Ka'ba, directly opposite the current door of the Ka'ba.
The meaning of the word al-mustajar is 'refuge' or 'sanctuary.' This place is known for the acceptance of repentance and is recommended for supplication and prayer.

Lady Khadija's House, (Arabic: دار السيدة خديجة, Dār al-Sayyida Khadīja), in Mecca, was the house where the Prophet(s) lived after marrying Lady Khadija until he migrated to Medina. This house was the birthplace of Lady Fatima(a) and other children of Khadija, and many Meccan verses of the Quran were also revealed to the Prophet(s) in that house, and also Khadija died in this house. Since the 6th/12th century, this place has been also known in sources as Lady Fatima's birthplace. Since then, there has been two domes in this house. One was called Qubbat al-Wahy and it was built on a room that was considered the place of worship of the Prophet(s) and the place where Gabriel descended on the Prophet. The other dome was built over a room that was said to be Lady Fatima's birthplace.
In the 14th/20th century, after House of Saud conquered Mecca, this house was destroyed and later a building for teaching the Quran was built in its place. During the demolition and excavation of this place to develop al-Masjid al-Haram in 1410/1989, the remains of the old building of Khadija's house were found under the soil. This place has disappeared today and its location is in the area of al-Masjid al-Haram.

Safa and Marwah are two small hills located on the eastern side of Masjid al-Haram. The distance between these two hills is called Mas'a, which is the place where one of the rites of Hajj and Umrah, known as Sa'i between Safa and Marwah, is performed.
According to historical narrations, Hagar (Hājar), the wife of Prophet Abraham (Ibrāhīm), ran between these two hills in search of water for her son Ishmael (Ismā'īl).
Mount Safa was also the starting point of the public call to Islam by Prophet Muhammad (PBUH). On the day of the conquest of Mecca, the Prophet ascended this hill and announced the good news of the Muslims' victory to the people.
The names of these two hills are mentioned in the Qur'an, and Islamic narrations emphasize their significance as one of the symbols (Sha‘ā'ir) of Islam, and the great reward for performing Sa'i between them.

Masjid al-Jinn, also known as Masjid al-Haras, is one of the ancient mosques in Mecca, located near the Al-Hajun cemetery. It is famously believed that Surah Al-Jinn was revealed to the Prophet Muhammad (PBUH) at this location, or that the Prophet (PBUH) met with the jinn in this mosque. The exact date of the mosque's original construction is unknown, but since the 2nd century AH, it has been known by this name and has undergone multiple repairs and renovations over the years.
In its latest renovation under the Saudi government, the mosque was adorned with a golden dome and a large minaret.

Wadi Muhassir is a region located between Muzdalifah and Mina, through which pilgrims pass during Hajj rituals. Muslim jurists have differed on whether this area is part of Mina or an independent region, but they all agree that offering a sacrifice there is valid, just like in Mina.
One of the rulings regarding Wadi Muhassir is that it is forbidden to pass through it before sunrise. This rule specifically applies to those who leave Muzdalifah heading to Mina on the morning of Eid al-Adha. It is also recommended for pilgrims to pass through this valley while hastening their pace.
There are signboards marking the boundaries of Wadi Muhassir, and its distance to Masjid al-Haram is 11 kilometers.
According to Shiite and Sunni jurists, Wadi Muhassir lies outside both Mina and Muzdalifah, and these three areas follow consecutively in order: "Mina", "Muhassir", and "Muzdalifah".
However, some jurists from both sects, based on certain narrations, consider Wadi Muhassir as part of Mina.

Al-Mashʿar (Arabic: المشعر), also known as al-Mashʿar al-Ḥarām (المشعر الحرام) or Muzdalifa (Arabic: مزدلفة) is a region between 'Arafat and Mina which has a length of about four km and staying there on the morning of Eid al-Adha is one of the essentials of hajj. Most hajj pilgrims collect stones for ramy al-jamarat from this place.
Gathering of hajis in Muzdalifa and around its mosque
This land which is located inside the Haram is a small valley between 'Arafat and Mina with the size of about four km. There is a large mosque in this area called Muzdalifa Mosque. The primary size of this mosque has been 1700 square meters,[1] which reached 4000 square meters at the time of Abbasids. That time, the mosque did not have a roof and there was only a fence around it. After many times of reconstruction and expansion, now its rectangular size is about 6000 square meters.
In verses 198 and 199 of Sura al-Baqara, the name of Muzdalifa is mentioned as al-Mash'ar al-Haram and people have been asked to remember God in this place.

Mina (Arabic: مِنَیٰ) is a plain located between the holy city of Mecca and al-Mash'ar al-Haram which is a part of Haram. From the morning of Eid al-Adha to two days later, pilgrims of hajj perform some rituals of hajj in this land. These rituals are: ramy al-jamarat, sacrificing, halq or taqsir, baytuta (stay at night) in Mina.
Various reasons have been reported for naming Mina (lit. wish). According to a report from Imam al-Rida (a), its name was taken from a phrase that the archangel Gabriel told Prophet Ibrahim (a): "Wish whatever you want from your Lord!" Ibrahim (a) asked God to send down a ram to be sacrificed instead of his son, Isma'il (a), and God fulfilled his wish.

Ramy al-Jamarāt (Arabic: رمي الجمرات) (Throwing pebbles) is one of the compulsory acts of Hajj rituals and it consists of throwing seven pebbles at three walls (formerly obelisks) that symbolize Satan. The act is performed on the day of Eid al-Adha and the next two days in Mina. Ramy al-Jamarat is a symbolic emulation of the Prophet Ibrahim's act.
Jamara (pl. Jamarat and Jimar) is an Arabic word meaning: A piece of blazing fire, pebbles etc. In Islamic terminology, Jamarat or al-Jamart al-Thalath (three) are the name of three certain places in Mina. Jamarat are known by three obelisks (currently walls). They are called Jamarat because they are the place where thrown pebbles pile up or because the pebbles are thrown at them or because people gather around them. In addition, Ramy means to throw or to hurl.
It is discussed in jurisprudential texts that if Jamara means the standing pillar, the pebbles must hit the pillar; but if it means the place where the pebbles are piled at the bottom of the pillar, hitting the pillars is not necessary.

Al-Khayf Mosque (Arabic:مسجد الخيف) is the most important mosque in the land of Mina in Mecca where the Prophet (s) performed prayer and delivered one of the speeches of Hajjat al-Wada' there. The name of this mosque has also been mentioned in al-Simat supplication. Today, this mosque which has been reconstructed in twenty five thousand square meter is open to those who make prayer only during the time of hajj.

Ḥirāʾ (Arabic: حراء) is one of the famous mountains in Mecca where the Prophet (s) has received his first revelation, resulting it's another name Jabal al-Nūr (the Light Mount). The mountain houses a cave known as cave of Hira' where the Prophet (s) used to spent great deal of time every year to engage himself in solitary worshiping before Islam (tahannuth) and it was there that he was honored with prophethood.

Mount Thawr (Arabic: جبل ثور) is a famous mountain in Mecca. The fame of this mountain is because of the cave where the prophet (s) at the beginning of Hijra (the Immigration) in Laylat al-Mabit sought shelter there from the polytheists. Polytheists went to the door of the cave, but thanks to the divine assistance, they could not find the Prophet (s).

Dhī Ṭuwā (Arabic: ذي طُوی) is a place in Mecca within the limits of the haram. According to some Shiite hadiths, it is the place where Imam al-Mahdi (a) lives during his Occultation and the place from which he will reappear before his uprising.
According to a hadith, before Imam al-Mahdi (a) begins his uprising from the Ka'ba, he will wait for his 313 companions in Dhi Tuwa. The place is mentioned in the Supplication of Nudba:
"I wish I knew where you are settled? and in which land to look for you? are you in Radwa? Or in some place else, or in Dhi Tuwa?"
Dhi Tuwa was located one parasang away from Mecca. Today, it is located on the way to Tan'im within Mecca. It is said that Prophet Muhammad (s) spent the eve of the Conquest of Mecca in this area. A mosque was later built where the Prophet (s) had camped, but it was destroyed because of the expansion of Tan'im street. It is located on the north of Masjid al-Haram. Tuwa water well (bi'r Tuwa) is a well-known place in the area. It is said that the Prophet (s) performed a wudu' with the water of this well.

Masjid al-Bay'ah (Bay'ah Mosque) is an ancient mosque in the city of Mecca, dating back to the 2nd century AH. This mosque is located near Jamarat al-Aqaba and outside the legal boundary of Mina. It is the place where the people of Yathrib pledged allegiance to the Prophet (s).
According to an inscription on the western wall of Masjid al-Bay'ah, the mosque was built in 144 AH/761-2 by the order of Mansur Abbasi and was later renovated several times.
When the people of Yathrib (Medina) became acquainted with Islam, during the days of Hajj, at the Jamarat al-Aqabah, they pledged allegiance to the prophet(s) for the first time. After that, Islam spread in Medina. At the site of this event, which is known as the First Pledge of Aqabah, a mosque was built that became famous as "Masjid al-Bay'ah" (Mosque of the Pledge).In sources, other mosques are also referred to by the name Masjid al-Bay'ah:
Masjid al-Ghanam, a mosque in Mecca, which was the place where the people of Mecca pledged allegiance to the Prophet (s).
Masjid al-Jinn, a mosque in Mecca, which is the place where the jinn pledged allegiance to the Prophet (s).

description.en: Taqsir means trimming a small amount of hair and nails
Tawaf al-Nisa’ is the act of circling the Kaaba seven times with the intention of Tawaf al-Nisa’. It is performed after Taqsir (trimming the hair) or Halq (shaving the head).

subtitle.en: which is the time for the Maghrib prayer

description.en: It means staying in the plain of Arafat. Its time is from noon on the ninth of Dhu al-Hijjah (the Day of Arafah) until the lawful sunset

subtitle.en: 10th of Dhu al-Hijjah: Staying in Muzdalifah (Mash'ar)

description.en: It means staying in Muzdalifah. The time for this act is from the dawn of the tenth of Dhu al-Hijjah (Eid al-Adha) until sunrise.

subtitle.en: one must keep the Kaaba to their left side at all times.

description.en: Tawaf is the act of circumambulating the Kaaba seven times in Masjid al-Haram.    It is obligatory that the seven rounds begin and end at the corner of the Kaaba where the Black Stone (Hajar al-Aswad) is located. While performing Tawaf

subtitle.en: and it must be performed with the intention of Tawaf al-Nisa’.

description.en: The prayer is performed behind Maqam Ibrahim (a). The prayer of Tawaf al-Nisa’ is like the prayer of Tawaf
